data.title: SentinelOne gains after bullish pitch from Citron Research
data.description:
SentinelOne (S) rose 4% after a bullish post from Citron Research.

"SentinelOne is time to pound the table," Citron wrote in a posting on X on Tuesday.  "Clawdbot is the tell- Autonomous Agents, the conversation has just begun, and $S has the lead in protecting the house." 

SentinelOne (S) shares have dropped 37% over the past year. 

This isn't the first time Citron has pitched SentinelOne (S). Last month, Citron said the company remains undervalued following its transition from an endpoint security vendor to an AI-native data and security platform. At the time, Citron set a $32 price target on the stock.

data.title: NBT Bancorp signals margin stability and expands Western New York presence following Evans merger
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NBT Bancorp Inc. (NBTB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Scott Kingsley reported that "our operating performance for the fourth quarter continued to reflect the positive attributes of productive fixed rate asset repricing trends, the diversification of our revenue streams, prudent balance sheet growth and the additive impact of our merger with Evans Bancorp completed in the second quarter."
  • Kingsley indicated operating return on assets was 1.37% and return on tangible equity was 17.02%, with tangible book value per share at $26.54 at year-end, up 11% year-over-year. He emphasized "growth in noninterest income continues to be a highlight with each of our nonbanking businesses achieving record results in both revenue and earnings generation for 2025."
  • Kingsley announced the repurchase of 250,000 shares in the fourth quarter and noted the company's "transition and integration activities over the past 8 months with the team members who joined us from Evans Bank have been highly successful."
  • Executive VP & CFO Annette Burns stated, "for the fourth quarter, we reported net income of $55.5 million or $1.06 per diluted common share." Burns added, "revenue generation remained favorable and consistent with the prior quarter and grew 25% from the fourth quarter of the prior year, driven by improvements in both net interest income and noninterest income, including the impact of the Evans merger."

Outlook

  • Kingsley addressed loan growth expectations, stating, "I think... the mid- to lower single-digit growth rate [is] a good number for '26," while noting the presence of an $800 million runoff loan portfolio moving downward about $100 million a year.
  • Burns commented on margin expectations: "Our net interest margin for the fourth quarter decreased 1 basis point to 3.65% compared with the prior quarter... the opportunity for further upward movement and earning asset yields will depend on the shape of the yield curve and how we reinvest loan and investment portfolio cash flows."
  • Kingsley stated that M&A remains part of capital deployment, explaining, "we're a $16 billion bank now... do we put our folks, our organization through an M&A opportunity that can't at least generate or 5% accretion?"

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q4 was $55.5 million or $1.06 per diluted share. Operating earnings, excluding acquisition-related expenses and securities gains, were $1.05 per share, consistent with the prior quarter.
  • Total loans, including those acquired from Evans, increased by $1.63 billion for the year. The total loan portfolio reached $11.6 billion, with 56% in commercial relationships and 44% in consumer loans.
  • Deposits rose by $2 billion from December 2024, with 58% of the portfolio consisting of no and low-cost checking and savings accounts.
  • Net interest income for the quarter was $135.4 million, up $1 million from the prior quarter.
  • Noninterest income was $49.6 million, a decrease of $1.8 million compared to the seasonally high third quarter, but up 17.4% year-over-year.
  • Operating expenses, excluding acquisition expenses, were $112 million, a 1.5% increase from the prior quarter.
  • Provision expense for loan losses was $3.8 million, up from $3.1 million in Q3, with reserves at 1.19% of total loans.

Q&A

  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group: Asked about CRE payoffs and loan growth outlook. Kingsley responded that $150 million to $175 million of unscheduled commercial real estate payoffs occurred in 2025 and noted risk of similar payoffs in 2026. He described pipelines as "stronger than they were at this point last year" and expressed bullishness on Western New York opportunities.
  • Mark Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler: Inquired about reserves in the solar loan book. Burns replied, "No fundamental change there... really, what you saw this quarter is kind of recalibration of that coverage ratio, but no trends or negative concerns as it relates to that book."
  • Fitzgibbon also asked about impact of U.S.-Canada tensions. Kingsley acknowledged "the unpredictability... caused hesitation and future investments" from Canadian customers, but noted these are not the highest growth areas.
  • Fitzgibbon questioned M&A hurdle rates. Kingsley stated, "do we put our folks, our organization through an M&A opportunity that can't at least generate or 5% accretion?... more generally, a modest extension of the franchise geographically or really productive fill-in opportunity."
  • Thomas Reed, Raymond James: Asked about hiring and expense impact. Kingsley and Senior Executive VP Joseph Stagliano detailed "adding good quality bankers" in Maine, Western New York, and plans for new branches in New England and Rochester.
  • David Konrad, KBW: Asked about NIM outlook. Burns explained, "net interest margin 3.65% is a very strong NIM... margin expansion, it's probably in that 2 or 3 basis points a quarter."
  • Daniel Cardenas, Janney Montgomery Scott: Asked about competition and deposit repricing. Kingsley noted some "defense when people have really solid customers" but no irrational competition; deposit cost reductions have a "lag" compared to Fed rate moves.
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: Inquired about charge-offs and the solar book. Burns described a new normalized charge-off rate in the "15 to 20 basis points range... as those books become smaller and smaller." Kingsley said selling the solar portfolio is limited by prevailing low rates.
  • Breese asked about share repurchases. Kingsley explained repurchases were "self-funded in the quarter and didn't change any of our capital ratios."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about CRE payoffs, loan growth, competition, deposit cost dynamics, and credit quality, with a slightly cautious but constructive tone. Several questions explored margin sustainability and M&A discipline.
  • Management's tone during prepared remarks was confident and highlighted operational success, while in Q&A, it remained upbeat but acknowledged risks, with phrases such as "we think that's an outsized number, but we're planning for -- that could be a risk for our growth attributes going forward this year" (Kingsley) and "no fundamental change" (Burns). Confidence was evident in statements about market opportunities and integration success.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone was similarly focused on growth and expense trends, but Q4 featured more direct questions on NIM and loan runoff risks. Management's confidence remained steady, with slightly more discussion of risk mitigation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 saw consistent operating earnings per share compared to Q3, with similar net interest margin and continued diversification of revenue streams.
  • Management reiterated the impact of Evans Bancorp integration, highlighting successful team onboarding and market expansion, especially in Western New York and New England.
  • Guidance on loan growth shifted slightly, with more explicit acknowledgment of commercial real estate payoff risks and mid- to lower-single-digit growth targets for 2026.
  • Analysts continued to focus on margin outlook, competition, and balance sheet dynamics, mirroring Q3 concerns. However, Q4 included additional dialogue on share repurchases and competitive deposit repricing.
  • Management's tone remained confident, with more direct references to risk factors and continued emphasis on capital strength and disciplined M&A.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged commercial real estate payoffs as a risk to loan growth, with Kingsley noting risks of "$150 million to $175 million of unscheduled commercial real estate payoffs" recurring.
  • Burns described recalibration of reserves in the solar loan book but reported "no trends or negative concerns."
  • Kingsley highlighted possible hesitancy among Canadian customers due to cross-border tensions, though this is not a primary growth area.
  • Management is monitoring hospitality and office space lending but reported no new emerging risks.
  • Analyst concerns centered on NIM sustainability, competition, deposit repricing, and the pace of loan runoff.

Final Takeaway

Management reported that NBT Bancorp's fourth quarter performance reflected stable operating earnings, solid net interest margin, and successful integration of Evans Bancorp, with continued growth in noninterest income and strategic market expansion. The company emphasized disciplined capital deployment, including opportunistic share repurchases, while planning for mid- to lower-single-digit loan growth amid ongoing commercial real estate payoff risks and evolving market dynamics. The leadership team remains confident in the bank's ability to generate positive operating leverage and pursue growth opportunities across its footprint.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. Gulf Coast crude oil, LNG exports fell to zero on Sunday due to freeze
data.description:
Exports of crude oil and liquefied natural gas from U.S. Gulf Coast ports fell to zero on Sunday, due to the massive winter storm that swept across the country, before exports rebounded on Monday, Reuters reported Tuesday, citing the Vortexa ship tracking service.

As much as 2M bbl/day of oil production, or ~15% of total U.S. output, went offline over the weekend due to the frigid weather, according to some analyst forecasts.

The Permian Basin alone saw production outages of ~1.5M bbl/day, according to estimates by Energy Aspects, although production is already recovering and could be fully restored by the end of the month.

Exports of liquefied petroleum gas, such as propane and butane, dropped to around a third of seasonal norms due to the storm, Vortexa's head of market analysis said.

In natural gas output, the winter storm led to losses of up to 11% of U.S. production, Rystad Energy said.

The total impact on January output suggests U.S. natural gas production would decline by 3.3B cf/day compared to an average of 104B cf/day for the Lower 48 region expected earlier in January, Rystad said.

Among U.S. companies with exposure to LNG: Cheniere Energy (LNG), Venture Global (VG), New Fortress Energy (NFE), NextDecade (NEXT) and Exxon Mobil (XOM).

data.title: Brown & Brown outlines 2026 margin target increase to 32%-37% with Accession integration and $30M-$40M synergy projection
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Brown & Brown, Inc. (BRO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO J. Powell Brown opened the call noting, "The fourth quarter capped off another year of strong top and bottom line financial performance. For the full year, we grew our revenue by 23% through a combination of M&A, organic revenue growth and strong growth in our contingent commissions." He highlighted the completion of the largest acquisition in company history with Accession, welcoming over 5,000 new teammates and indicating satisfaction with integration progress.

  • Brown addressed competitive pressures, stating, "As of today, approximately 275 of our former teammates have joined this start-up, taking with them customers currently representing known annual revenues of $23 million. As we've done in the past, we will defend our rights in court and already have obtained an injunction." He emphasized a continued customer-centric and disciplined approach.

  • CFO R. Watts reported, "For the quarter, Accession's total revenue was approximately $405 million. This is below the guidance of $430 million to $450 million. As a result of refining our revenue recognition estimates by quarter, revenue, margins and adjusted earnings per share were impacted for the quarter. However, these revisions do not change our annual expectations for the business."

  • Watts further explained, "Our EBITDAC margin was 32.9%, remaining flat versus the fourth quarter of the prior year. This was a good result considering the negative 200 basis point impact of Accession mentioned earlier and the prior year flood claim processing revenue."

Outlook

  • Watts stated, "For next year, we anticipate contingents for Specialty Distribution will be down approximately $15 million due to certain onetime adjustments in 2025 and ultimately subject to storm claim activity."

  • Watts provided guidance: "For Specialty Distribution, we anticipate organic growth to be somewhat flat in the first quarter due to flood claims processing revenue in the first quarter of last year and continued CAT property rate decreases. As it relates to 2026 organic revenue outlook for the Retail segment, we'd anticipate modest improvement over the 2.8% we delivered in 2025."

  • The company increased its long-term adjusted EBITDAC margin target range to 32% to 37% from the previous 30% to 35%.

  • Watts shared, "We expect EBITDA synergies of approximately $30 million to $40 million in 2026" from Accession integration efforts.

  • Brown offered a macro perspective: "Assuming interest rates continue to decrease, in 2026, this should provide additional economic stimulus for many companies as well as individuals."

Financial Results

  • Brown reported, "For the fourth quarter, we delivered revenues of $1.6 billion, growing 35.7% in total with organic revenue decreasing 2.8%, driven substantially by flood claims processing revenue we recognized in the fourth quarter of last year. Our adjusted EBITDAC margin remained flat at 32.9% and our adjusted earnings per share grew over 8% to $0.93."

  • Watts highlighted, "On a consolidated basis, we delivered total revenues of $1.607 billion, growing 35.7% as compared to the fourth quarter 2024. Contingent commissions grew by an impressive $37 million, with $21 million coming from Accession."

  • Watts noted, "For the full year of '25, we delivered revenues of $5.9 billion, growing 23% in total and 2.8% organically. Our adjusted EBITDAC margin was approximately 36%, increasing 70 basis points."

  • Watts described strong cash performance: "We generated $1.450 billion of cash flow from operations, growing 23.5% over the prior year."

Q&A

  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: Questioned changes in retention strategy and legal defenses after the departure of 275 teammates. Brown responded that compensation structures remain unchanged and, "there are nonpiracy and nonsolicitation agreements... typically have a 2-year period on customers and a 2-year period on hiring teammates. It also has a component on intellectual property, which is in perpetuity."

  • Jamminder Bhullar, JPMorgan: Asked about the shift of business from E&S to standard. Brown explained that movement is most evident in "smaller accounts" and is not yet a trend. Watts added, "While accounts can, in fact, migrate from the E&S back into admitted, we continue to believe that there's going to be more insured assets though moving into the E&S space versus moving back into the admitted."

  • Robert Cox, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about casualty pricing deceleration. Brown stated, "What you're seeing, at least in primary business is a slight moderation of those rate increases. Remember, the biggest pressure in that area is on the excess. That has not changed."

  • Yaron Kinar, Mizuho: Asked about offset drivers to Specialty Distribution growth headwinds. Watts replied, "We start getting the benefit of the organic growth of the Specialty Distribution businesses that have joined us from a session... there's quite a bit of casualty plus other specialty lines inside."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on competitive risks, retention of talent, and pricing trends, displaying a neutral to slightly negative tone when probing about the impact of lost employees and revenue, as well as pressure on margins and organic growth.

  • Management maintained a confident and steady tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A. Brown emphasized discipline and stability, while Watts consistently described performance as strong and outlook as positive, even when addressing challenges: "We feel really good about our prospects for 2026."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence was sustained, but there was a more direct acknowledgment of competitive threats and associated legal actions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Organic revenue growth moderated, with Q4 showing a decrease of 2.8%, compared to 3.5% organic growth in Q3.

  • Margin performance remained solid but was affected by Accession integration and lower than guided revenue contribution from Accession in Q4.

  • Analyst focus shifted more heavily toward competitive poaching and the resulting business risk, while last quarter's questions centered on integration and segment outlook.

  • Management's tone remained stable, but there was increased discussion of defensive measures and the impact of external competitive actions on reported results.

Risks and Concerns

  • Loss of 275 employees to a competitor, resulting in $23 million in lost revenue, with potential for further impact as the situation evolves.

  • Continued softness in CAT property rates and pressure on organic growth in Specialty Distribution segment.

  • Dependency on contingent commissions, which are expected to decrease by approximately $15 million in 2026 due to onetime adjustments.

  • Ongoing litigation and non-solicitation enforcement related to team departures.

  • Competitive pressures leading to possible margin impacts and talent retention challenges.

Final Takeaway

Brown & Brown's Q4 2025 showcased robust top and bottom line growth for the year, bolstered by the Accession acquisition and a strong cash flow profile. The company increased its long-term margin target to 32%-37% and expects $30 million to $40 million in EBITDA synergies from the Accession integration in 2026. While competitive poaching and softer CAT property rates present near-term headwinds, management remains confident in the stability of its operating model, disciplined capital allocation, and the ability to drive continued growth and shareholder value in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AGNC signals continued constructive agency MBS backdrop as portfolio leverage and hedge strategy evolve
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Peter Federico, President, CEO & Chief Investment Officer, emphasized AGNC's strong results in 2025, noting "AGNC's 11.6% economic return in the fourth quarter drove our impressive full year economic return of 22.7%. Even more noteworthy, AGNC's total stock return in 2025 was 34.8% with dividends reinvested, nearly double the performance of the S&P 500." He highlighted the stability in mortgage spreads and the constructive investment environment emerging as the Fed shifted to a more accommodative stance. Federico stated that AGNC is "very well positioned to generate compelling risk-adjusted returns with a substantial yield component for our shareholders."
  • Bernice Bell, Executive VP & CFO, reported, "For the fourth quarter, AGNC reported comprehensive income of $0.89 per common share. Our economic return on tangible common equity was 11.6% for the quarter, consisting of $0.36 of dividends declared per common share and a $0.60 increase in tangible net book value per share driven by lower interest rate volatility and tighter mortgage spreads to benchmark interest rates." Bell also noted, "We ended the fourth quarter with leverage of 7.2x tangible equity, down from 7.6x at the end of the third quarter."
  • Federico explained that the company shifted its hedge mix toward a greater proportion of interest rate swaps, with the allocation to swap-based hedges increasing to 70% of the portfolio from 59% the prior quarter.

Outlook

  • Management expects lower funding costs from the October and December rate cuts and anticipated future cuts, increased stability in funding markets, and the shift in the hedge mix to provide a "moderate tailwind to net spread and dollar roll income."
  • Federico stated, "as the largest pure-play agency mortgage REIT, we believe AGNC is very well positioned to generate compelling risk-adjusted returns with a substantial yield component for our shareholders."
  • The supply and demand outlook for agency MBS in 2026 is expected to remain well balanced, with private sector absorption of about $400 billion in MBS and potential for GSE purchases to consume about half of this supply.

Financial Results

  • Comprehensive income was reported at $0.89 per common share for Q4 2025. Economic return on tangible common equity stood at 11.6% for the quarter.
  • Tangible net book value per common share increased by $0.60. Monthly dividends declared totaled $0.36 per share for the quarter.
  • Leverage decreased to 7.2x tangible equity by quarter end. Liquidity was strong with $7.6 billion in cash and unencumbered Agency MBS, representing 64% of tangible equity.
  • Net spread and dollar roll income was unchanged for the quarter at $0.35 per common share.
  • The asset portfolio totaled $95 billion at quarter end, up $4 billion from the prior quarter, with 76% of assets having favorable prepayment attributes.

Q&A

  • Bose George, KBW: Asked about current spreads and dividend coverage. Federico responded, "mortgage spreads... have now sort of entered a new spread range," estimating current coupon to swaps in the 120 to 160 range. He elaborated, "the return on our existing portfolio... aligns very, very well with our total cost of capital."
  • Douglas Harter, UBS: Inquired about spread risks and leverage. Federico explained that spread stability is key for leverage decisions, stating, "we have let our leverage come down consistent with the spread tightening."
  • Crispin Love, Piper Sandler: Asked about the administration's housing affordability efforts. Federico credited the administration and GSEs for "the actions that they took in 2025" and emphasized the importance of continued focus on mortgage market stability.
  • Trevor Cranston, Citizens JMP: Asked about swap spreads and volatility. Federico indicated the outlook is "favorable for swap spreads" and expects interest rate volatility to remain generally low.
  • Jason Stewart, Compass Point: Queried about equity issuance and demand outside GSEs. Federico reported no equity issuance quarter-to-date and noted a "more diverse investor base" for agency MBS, with strong bond fund inflows and expected growth in bank and foreign demand.
  • Richard Shane, JPMorgan: Clarified ATM issuance timing. Federico confirmed, "most companies... in a blackout period from the end of the previous period to sometime around their earnings call."
  • Eric Hagen, BTIG: Asked about prepayment speeds and hedging. Federico highlighted the importance of asset selection and maintaining a positive duration gap, noting "specified pool characteristics are going to be really important."
  • Harsh Hemnani, Green Street: Explored coupon composition and duration gap. Federico stated, "there is ample liquidity for us to position the portfolio anyway we want from an overall coupon distribution perspective," and noted a current duration gap of about 0.5 years.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of returns, spread levels, and capital deployment, with a tone that was neutral to slightly positive as they probed both upside and risk scenarios.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing alignment of returns with capital costs and the favorable outlook for agency MBS, while acknowledging potential risks and the need for ongoing monitoring of market conditions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident but with more emphasis on the need for spread stability and careful leverage management given the evolving environment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from highlighting the benefits of post-Fed pivot conditions in Q3 to emphasizing the sustainability of spread ranges and the impact of GSE actions in Q4.
  • Leverage decreased from 7.6x to 7.2x tangible equity, and the hedge mix increased toward swap-based hedges (70% vs. 59%).
  • Management’s tone evolved from optimism about new capital deployment and demand growth to a more nuanced focus on spread and leverage stability amid a favorable yet dynamic environment.
  • Analysts in both quarters pressed on dividend sustainability and risk, but Q4 saw deeper exploration of policy and demand drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified potential negative impacts from policy changes such as streamlined refinance programs, g-fees, or mortgage portability, which could "have negative consequences, some of them significantly negative consequences."
  • Prepayment risk is higher given the direction of the administration, and asset selection is seen as critical to performance.
  • Federico cautioned that leverage decisions depend on "how stable do we believe spreads will be" and highlighted the importance of monitoring government actions.

Final Takeaway

AGNC management highlighted its strong 2025 results and the favorable agency MBS environment heading into 2026, supported by a constructive macro backdrop, robust liquidity, and prudent risk management. The company remains focused on maintaining spread stability, optimizing portfolio composition, and leveraging a diversified investor base to drive sustained returns and reliable dividend coverage for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: UnitedHealth eyes benefit reductions after “disappointing” Medicare rate notice
data.description:
Following a Q4 report that didn't fully reflect Street expectations, UnitedHealth (UNH) plunged ~19%, mainly in reaction to what the company identified as “disappointing” reimbursement rates proposed by the Trump administration for Medicare Advantage payers in 2027.

However, the Eden Prairie, Minnesota-based health insurer, the number one player in the Medicare Advantage market, implied it was looking at benefit reductions as an option while it seeks an improved final rate in negotiations with the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services ((CMS)).

UnitedHealth (UNH) and its peers, Humana (HUM), CVS Health (CVS), Molina (MOH), and Elevance Health (ELV), plunged in post-market trading on Monday after the CMS issued the 2027 MA Advance Notice, which proposed flat reimbursement rates for next year.

The stocks came under renewed selling pressure on Tuesday after UnitedHealth (UNH), the earnings bellwether for the sector, reported Q4 2025 results and indicated lower-than-expected revenue for 2026, marking its first annual revenue decline in more than three decades.

“The news that we received last night in the advance notice was disappointing,” said Tim Noel, CEO of UNH’s insurance arm, UnitedHealthcare. “So we're going to, of course, work with CMS from now until the rates are finalized.”

“But as this all sits today, as I talked about, it will mean very meaningful benefit reductions,” Noel added, noting that the company will have to review its business operations in terms of its geographic footprint and product portfolio.

“We don't see this to be something that's going to be broadly disproportionate payer by payer; therefore, seniors kind of across the sector are going to experience these implications of reduced choice, reduced access, and affordability challenges.”

data.title: S&P 500 tops new record, flirts with 7,000 level as investors stay focused on big tech earnings
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Tuesday, with the S&P 500 ((SP500)) achieving a new intraday record, getting very close to the 7,000 level.

Traders' optimism was derived from important tech companies reporting this week, including Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Tesla (TSLA), and Apple (AAPL).

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.5% in midday trade, while the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was +1%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.6%.

“Performance disparities in technology (XLK) aren’t limited to geography as they’re equally prevalent across U.S. market cap ranges; it ranks as second-worst performer among large-caps YTD while it’s outperforming 3% in small-cap space; conversely, communication services (XLC) shows opposite: S&P 500 (SP500) sector leads its parent index by 1.3%, whereas the S&P SmallCap 600 (SP600) version lags small-cap benchmark by 5.7%,” said Liz Ann Sonders, chief investment strategist at Schwab Center for Financial Research.

On Monday, U.S. President Donald Trump announced he would be slapping 25% tariffs on South Korean autos, lumber, pharmaceuticals, and other goods, up from 15%.

On the other hand, a U.S.-India trade deal was confirmed on Tuesday, which would gradually cut tariffs on most imports.

In addition, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) continues reaching new highs, touching almost $5,100 on Tuesday.

“Everyone asks why gold is hitting records. Simple: central banks don’t trust their own money anymore. When the issuers run for gold, you should probably pay attention,” said Marc-André Fongern, former Deutsche Bank and Goldman Sachs investment banker.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point higher to 4.23%, while the short-end 2-year yield (US2Y) slipped 2 basis points to 3.58%.

On interest rates, the Federal Reserve is expected to keep rates the same at 3.5%-3.75% during its Jan. 27-28 meeting. According to the CME FedWatch Tool, 97.2% of investors are sure of it.

data.title: Sonos introduces a new product after a long pause to reset strategy
data.description:
Sonos (SONO) introduced a new product for the first time in more than a year on Tuesday. 

The Amp Multi product was described as a professional-grade, rack-mountable multi-channel streaming amplifier aimed at advanced users and custom installers rather than the mass market. The new component can power up to four independently configurable Sonos zones and eight amplified outputs from a single unit, making it well-suited for larger, luxury homes where the hardware is hidden in an equipment rack while driving traditional in-wall or in-ceiling speakers across the property.

Ampi Multi, which is positioned within Sonos' (SONO) components business alongside products like Amp and Port, is designed to deepen adoption of the Sonos (SONO) platform in whole-home installations and embed the system into the architecture of the home instead of relying solely on visible, standalone speakers. 

The launch of Ampi Multi effectively marks the end of an intentional pause in hardware releases while new CEO Tom Conrad focused on fixing the troubled mobile app and rebuilding brand reputation following the botched software overhaul and the departure of former chief executive Patrick Spence. While Amp Multi itself is a niche, installer-focused product, it could serve as an early proof point for Conrad’s strategy to grow revenue by increasing the number of Sonos devices per household.

"We created Amp Multi with integrators at the heart of every decision, drawing on their deep understanding of real-world installation challenges to design a product around how they actually work," stated Chris Heintzelman, VP, Product at Sonos (SONO).

Shares of Sonos (SONO) are down 14.8% for the early part of 2026.

data.title: UPS outlines $3B cost savings target and 1M daily Amazon volume reduction for 2026 as network reconfiguration accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: United Parcel Service (UPS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Carol Tomé, CEO, began by expressing gratitude to the UPS team and addressing the response to the Flight 2976 tragedy. She stated, "Looking at the fourth quarter, our results exceeded our expectations, driven by strong revenue quality, solid cost management and overall great execution." Tomé highlighted all three business segments contributed to outperformance, with U.S. Domestic and Supply Chain Solutions expanding operating margins year-over-year, and International Small Package reporting its highest fourth quarter revenue in four years.
  • Tomé detailed significant progress in 2025, including reaching the Amazon volume reduction target with a decrease of approximately 1 million pieces per day, $3.5 billion in savings from network initiatives, closing 93 U.S. buildings, and automating 57. U.S. revenue per piece grew 7.1% year-over-year, and SMB penetration rose to 31.8% of total U.S. volume. The Digital Access Program grew revenue by 25% and B2B volume share increased by 250 basis points, hitting 42.3%. She also noted the completion of Frigo-Trans and Andlauer Healthcare Group acquisitions, with global healthcare portfolio revenue at $11.2 billion.
  • Tomé stated, "For the full year 2026, we intend to glide down another 1 million pieces per day [of Amazon volume] while continuing to reconfigure our network." She explained 2026 as a year with revenue and margin impacts in the first half from the Amazon glide-down and Ground Saver outsourcing, but expects a more efficient network and sequential growth in the back half.
  • Tomé shared that UPS plans to further automate its network, targeting 68% of U.S. volume processed through automated facilities by the end of 2026. The company will retire all MD-11 aircraft, replacing them with new Boeing 767s. A new agreement with the U.S. Postal Service for Ground Saver improves product economics.
  • Brian Dykes, CFO, reported, "In the fourth quarter, revenue was $24.5 billion and operating profit was $2.9 billion. Consolidated operating margin was 11.8% and diluted earnings per share were $2.38." Dykes stated that 2025 savings came from reducing 26.9 million labor hours, cutting 48,000 positions, and closing 93 buildings. He also confirmed a $137 million after-tax charge related to the MD-11 fleet retirement.

Outlook

  • Tomé stated that for 2026, UPS expects consolidated revenue of approximately $89.7 billion and a consolidated operating margin of approximately 9.6%. She described a "bathtub effect" with first half down and second half up for U.S. revenue and operating margin, expecting to exit 2026 with a leaner, more agile U.S. network.
  • Dykes explained that for 2026, "we intend to glide down another 1 million pieces per day of Amazon volume" and target $3 billion in savings related to the Amazon glide down. He indicated U.S. Domestic average daily volume will be down mid-single digits, offset by mid-single digit revenue per piece growth. Supply Chain Solutions revenue is expected up high single digits, and International revenue growth in the low single digits.

Financial Results

  • UPS reported Q4 consolidated revenue of $24.5 billion, operating profit of $2.9 billion, and a consolidated operating margin of 11.8%. Diluted earnings per share were $2.38. Full-year consolidated revenue was $88.7 billion, operating profit $8.7 billion, and operating margin 9.8%. Cash from operations reached $8.5 billion.
  • U.S. Domestic segment Q4 revenue was $16.8 billion, with operating profit of $1.7 billion and a 10.2% margin. Average daily volume declined 10.8%, while revenue per piece increased 8.3%. International reported $5 billion in revenue and $908 million in operating profit, with an 18% margin. Supply Chain Solutions posted $2.7 billion in revenue and $276 million in operating profit, with a 10.3% margin.

Q&A

  • David Vernon, Bernstein: Asked about exit rate margins and cost impact from MD-11 retirement. Dykes responded, "In the fourth quarter, included in our results was about $50 million of incremental lease costs...It will be about double that in 2026...90% of that in the first half." He added, "about 100 basis points of pressure in the first half that relieves in the second half."
  • Thomas Wadewitz, UBS: Inquired about post-Amazon glide-down algorithm and cost recovery from Ground Saver. Dykes indicated mid-single-digit enterprise and SMB growth in the back half, with "cost per piece will normalize as well...we expect to see cost per piece come down below rev per piece."
  • Ken Hoexter, BofA: Asked about rate increase trends. Dykes stated, "For the year...about 4.5% rev per piece growth."
  • Ariel Rosa, Citi: Sought clarity on cost per piece normalization. Dykes said, "We will see the cost per piece normalize to that kind of normal inflation level." Tomé noted, "The cost per piece in these automated buildings is 28% less than...conventional buildings."
  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: Asked about international segment pressures. Dykes noted, "We are going to see extreme weakness in the first quarter that...gradually recovers." Tomé added, "We were down 360 basis points in the fourth quarter."
  • Jordan Alliger, Goldman Sachs: Questioned drivers of second-half package growth. Tomé explained, "The U.S. small package market appears to be stabilizing...excluding Amazon...projected to grow in the low single digits."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on cost normalization, margin exit rates, and recovery timelines, reflecting a slightly skeptical to neutral tone. The questioning focused on cost timing, margin pressure, and the pace of benefit realization from strategic actions.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently reaffirming cost-out progress and automation benefits, but acknowledged transition challenges. Tomé said, "We are confident that we will be able to complete our network reconfiguration plans without impeding our ability to grow in targeted markets."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone is more forward-looking and assertive about the second-half inflection, while analysts remain cautious about near-term risks and cost recovery.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter features more detailed guidance for 2026, including explicit targets for Amazon volume reduction and cost savings. The previous quarter highlighted ongoing transformation and uncertainty around trade policy impacts, with less specificity on the next year’s cadence.
  • Management now emphasizes the "bathtub effect"—first half weakness followed by second half recovery—while the previous quarter focused on near-term cost-out and network reconfiguration milestones.
  • The tone has shifted to greater confidence in achieving structural margin expansion, supported by automation and product mix enhancements. Analyst questions have become more granular, probing timing and magnitude of cost and margin normalization, compared to broader transformation queries last quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, trade policy changes, and the execution risk of network reconfiguration and Amazon volume glide-down. Transition expenses and the lag in fixed cost reductions present near-term profit risks.
  • International segment faces continued pressure from trade lane mix shifts and tough comparisons due to tariff front running in 2025.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the pace of cost normalization, the impact of aircraft retirement and leases, and the timing of benefits from Ground Saver transition and automation.

Final Takeaway

UPS management outlined a pivotal year ahead, targeting another 1 million pieces per day reduction in Amazon volume and $3 billion in related cost savings for 2026. While near-term revenue and margin pressure are expected as the company completes its network reconfiguration and transitions Ground Saver delivery to USPS, the back half of the year is positioned as an inflection point, with anticipated margin improvement and growth in targeted segments like SMB, B2B, healthcare, and international. The combination of automation, disciplined capital allocation, and a more agile network is expected to deliver long-term structural profitability and value for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: FOMC preview: Fed seen hitting pause to reset the stage for 2026
data.description:
The Federal Open Market Committee is widely expected to pause its rate-cutting path on Wednesday, following three successive rate reductions. The question will be when the next cut will be, if there are any more cuts this year. Most FOMC makers appear to be in no rush to ease policy further.

The labor market, though softening, isn't flashing urgent warnings. Likewise, while inflation remains above the Federal Reserve's 2% goal, there's no indication of a dramatic resurgence. That gives the monetary policymakers "time to figure out what they want to do and set the stage for 2026," said Bankrate Financial Analyst Stephen Kates in an interview with Seeking Alpha.

"I think that from the Federal Open Market Committee's perspective, they need to let the dust settle a little bit," he said. 

In what would normally be a boring Fed meeting, with its benchmark rate expected to stay at 3.50%-3.75%, a flurry of issues swirling around the central bank, include: 

  • The upcoming change in Fed chair when Jerome Powell's term ends in May;
  • Renewed tariff threats that erupted with Trump's now withdrawn demands to gain control of Greenland;
  • An investigation into the central bank's renovation of its main buildings that Powell says is a pretext for pressuring the Fed to lower interest rates; and 
  • The Supreme Court's pending decision on whether President Trump can fire Fed governors at will.

Looking ahead, there's a disconnect between what the FOMC has signaled in terms of the number of projected rate cuts in 2026, as indicated in its most recent Summary of Economic Projections, and what markets are expecting.

"The summary of economic projections in both September and December showed that there were expectations of only a single rate cut in 2026," Bankrate's Kates said. "I think resetting market expectations about whether that's still the case and what the timing of that might be is going to be really important because market odds currently show the expectation of two Federal Reserve rate cuts this year."

In interest rate markets, traders expect a 97.2% probability that the FOMC will keep its rate unchanged at Wednesday's meeting, according to the CME FedWatch tool. The probability of a rate cut doesn't exceed 50% until June.

Prediction markets are also seeing at least two more pauses, with three more likely. On Polymarket, the probability that the Fed will cut its rate in April is 30%, with a 70% chance of a cut in June.

There's a wide range of views among economists and other observers. Allianz Trade and Bankrate both expect only one rate cut this year, while Citi's Andrew Hollenhorst expects three rate cuts. "The long-term trend continues to be toward gradually loosening labor markets and slowing inflation, and we continue to expect 75 bp of further rate cuts this year (in March, July, and September)," he wrote in a note to clients.

Allianz Trade, though, contends that "only accelerating productivity, easing labor costs (-0.5pp over the past year) in the context of the AI rollout and a decrease in tariffs would allow the Fed to cut key rates to 3% this year with inflation cooling faster."

Watch for any dissents from Fed policymakers. In the last two meetings, Fed Governor Stephen Miran dissented, preferring 50 basis-point rate cuts, in contrast with the 25-bp cuts the majority voted for. He has voiced his opinion that he believes the Fed should cut by 150 basis points this year. Fed Governor Christopher Waller estimates that the Fed rate is at least 50 bps above neutral, the point at which interest rates neither stimulate nor hinder economic growth.

While much of the focus has been on risks to the labor market, inflation remains a concern even as it has subsided from its peak in June 2022. Middle-market companies are especially feeling the pinch of higher prices, said Jill Gateman, head of U.S. Middle Market and Specialized Finance and co-head of Commercial Bank at TD Bank. "Many companies are still managing higher labor and input costs while demand remains cautious, and planning feels harder than it should," she said. "A pause gives the Fed time to see how earlier rate moves are really working their way through the economy, and it gives businesses a bit more clarity as they make decisions about investing, hiring, and managing liquidity in a very uncertain environment."

Bankrate's Kates observes that "we are not in the clear on inflation or labor, but we are pulling back from the precipice."

On the labor market side, the rate of payroll growth has reset as immigration is severely restricted and a “healthy number” of workers are "jumping into retirement every day," he said. With that, the point of labor market equilibrium (where fewer new jobs are needed to absorb those entering the labor force) may have declined to between 40K and 70K per month. That compared with 2024 and early 2025, when a monthly increase of 160K in payrolls was common.

22V Research's Peter Williams notes the absolute nonfarm payroll number may not be as crucial as it once was. "Given immigration and population aging, most on the FOMC seem increasingly skeptical of the importance of NFP growth rates (1 derivative) rather than their momentum or deterioration (2 derivative)," he wrote in a note to clients. "When in doubt, focus on the household survey and claims, for which these issues play a much smaller role."

His firm's baseline expects the next cut in June after the Fed's new chair takes over, "but recent political dynamics are heightening the odds of a less dramatically dovish new chair and a more resistant Fed," Williams added.

During the post-decision press conference, Powell will find it more difficult to deflect questions about political pressures on the central bank. Earlier this month, Powell issued a video regarding the Department of Justice's investigation into his oversight of the bank's renovation projects, saying the move is meant to punish the Fed for refusing to cut rates as much as President Trump wants.

Powell still has the option of staying on the Fed board after his term as chair ends, defying tradition. Kates, though, doesn't expect that the Fed chair will make any such announcement on Wednesday.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
